speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Margot Heuman or Yoyman-- Oyman-- Heuman. I got it. OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about the transport to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, my grand-- my maternal grandparents had six months before being transported over to Theresienstadt. And somehow we were able to correspond with them-- and I don't know how-- and send packages to them, which I found out later that they did receive. So they knew that we were coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we arrived in Theresienstadt-- by the way, the train stopped at many, many cities-- and I do not recall the names of the cities-- and picked up other people. So it was a journey that lasted like, two days. And it was a whole train filled with people that went to Theresienstadt. Passenger train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a passenger train, yeah. That was the only transportation I had in the passenger train during the war years. But there was a passenger train. And we arrived, arrived in Theresienstadt. [SIGHS] And I remember being in this big, huge hall. Now, Theresienstadt is a ghetto, and it was Jewish self-rule, but there were rules and regulations that had to be followed, given by the Germans. But there were very few Germans around. It was basically Jews that administered to the administration of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find your grandparents when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's the thing. My-- we found my grandmother. My grandfather had died. He had died, probably of starvation because he was a very big man. And three months after they arrived in Theresienstadt, he had died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how did they get to Theresienstadt? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why Theresienstadt? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: They--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they had come like, six months before from another city, from [NON-ENGLISH]. And actually, from my immediate family-- I'm just sidetracking-- but I had my father's father, and my father's brother and his wife had gone to Holland, but eventually he was sent to concentration camps-- I don't know which ones-- from Holland and were perished. They were-- died. Died during there. And my father's sister and her husband and three children were also sent to, I think, Dachau or somewhere like that from Germany, and they also died. And then there was my grandparents and my parents and I. And all the others were able to leave Germany except for my aunt, who stayed there. And I'm the only survivor from anyone who did not get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we saw my-- my mother was very upset because her father had died. My grandmother was skin and bones. My mother was-- I remember my mother crying hysterically. Everything was like, chaos about everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they took us to a barrack. It, it wasn't a barrack. It was like a big room in a barrack with a-- it has a-- it had a stone floor. And everybody was given this little space to put their clothing, their belongings. And I think we were given a straw mattress, and we had our own blankets I believe. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were living in the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was filled. I don't-- I mean, huge. And there were men, and women, and children. But they said, this was only like, a quarantine. We-- I mean, I don't know why we had to be quarantined since we were the healthy ones. But we were-- that was-- would only be for a short while, and then we would be moved to other quarters. And the short while lasted to be about a month. In the meantime, hunger. We were hungry. We didn't have enough to eat. There was no abuse. It was just that we were very, very hungry. And then I became-- I had fleas and lice. And we started scratching. And I got impetigo, and I was-- my whole body was an oozing sore. And my-- in the meantime, my father tried to get us into a youth home, which the Jewish organizations had set up because they really did give children extra privileges. And finally, we-- both my sister and I were able to get into this youth home. And I went into a room with 17 girls my own age, within a year or two. And my sister was in another room with 15 or 17. And we had bunk beds. We did get some milk every-- I don't know whether it was every day or every other day. We got a little more food. We did have a shower once a week. We-- so we-- it was better, much better than staying where we had been. My parents eventually moved to a smaller room with my grandmother. And they had-- again, so there were about 10 or 12 couples. But they had a little bigger space. The, the worst thing in Theresienstadt was hunger. There was no-- as far as I remember, there was no abuse. I remember having friends again. There would be teachers. We had some classes. The first opera I've ever heard was in Theresienstadt. I, I saw-- I heard We had Jewish musicians. We, we were all ardent Zionists. The whole-- I mean, all of us were going to go to Israel. I-- very, very pro Jewish. And being with all these young people, somehow life, it was tolerable. I mean, we were always hungry. We never had enough to eat. But, somehow, I think I, I-- it wasn't any worse than being in Germany and being emotionally abused from morning till night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I visited my parents at least three times a week. Since I had more food, I was able to give them some. Eventually I got a job. I marched out of the ghetto, and I worked in, in-- on a farm. It was called Landwirtschaft. And I was able to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. L-A-N-D-W-I-R-D-S-C-H-A-F-T. And I was able to eat spinach from where we were harvesting, harvesting it. So we just ate it. And then later on, we got very good at smuggling stuff back. And I brought some stuff back-- to my parents when I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you smuggle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stuff it into our-- into our blouses, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who, who were you working with?

speaker: interviewee
text: A group of young people from the-- from the home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I remember a lot of them because the ones in my room, I remember almost everyone. [SIGHS] My closest friend was Dita. Her name is Edith, but I called her Dita. And she came from Vienna. And we were, more or less, the same age. And we stayed-- we were actually together till the end, so she, she really was part of my whole life in the camp. My friend, Hilda, lives in Baltimore now. I still am in contact with her. My friend Karla lives in Israel, and I still am in contact with her. I have a friend, Rita, who lives in Rio de Janeiro. There are about eight or 10 of people I was with in New York, but I have no contact with them because we just grew apart. We're completely different people now.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all in your home-- in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all in the home with me, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your sister? Where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was in a home with young-- in the home, in a room with younger children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw her. She came to me for help, and I didn't want to help her. I told her she was bothering me. But I had my friend, Hilda, the woman who is in Baltimore now. She had-- she always was like a mother. I mean, she was like two years older than we were, so she helped my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she needed help sewing something or, or closing something. And I said, oh, don't bother me. So she-- Hilda helped her for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to celebrate holidays during-- in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did. We had-- we had-- we-- oh, once-- when we got milk, we used to beat it. And it made it very foamy. And it fills up your stomach so you're not so hungry. Once a week on Shabbat, we got [NON-ENGLISH], which is a little, a little cake-like thing. It's like a bread, but it's a sweet bread. And it had a chocolate sauce over it. And that was our good, good part. The rest of the time, we were-- we were always hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got it from, from, from the town. From the manager, from the Jewish manager.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate Shabbat with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I celebrated everything in the home. We stayed-- I got no food with my parents. I had to stay in the home. I visited my parents when I could. But basically, my life was in that home with all these young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in that home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably about seven out of 15,000, various age groups, male and female, children separated. A lot. And we had a all-- we had a lot of celebrations. We sang. We had Tikvah, had all kinds of lessons and lectures on Judaism. We learned [NON-ENGLISH], which I forgot most of, but we did learn it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave the lectures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Teachers. I mean, we had a lot of people that, that were there that had been teachers, I mean, very, very qualified. You know that some of the musicians composed music that the Boston Symphony has certain musicians that are now performing this music. I don't know whether you've heard about it. And I mean, these were very good musicians. They just happen to at Theresienstadt, and they loved music.

speaker: interviewer
text: There are a lot of cultural events?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Plays,

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides the opera--

speaker: interviewee
text: Shakespeare, [NON-ENGLISH]. Music, concerts, opera. We, we performed plays too. I mean, we wrote plays, performed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What types of plays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, just fun plays. Make believe, basically, how-- what life would be like if we were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- what did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we felt that we could be with our-- live with our families again, have enough to eat. That was the main thing. Take a hot bath, sleep in a bed-- things like that-- with a white sheet.

speaker: interviewer
text: You performed-- you performed these for other people in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just for the children among each other. Like they-- they competed. They had competitions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any romance ever in the house between people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were too young. Well, there was some, maybe. Not-- very innocent romances, I mean, in the kids that I was with. I mean-- how old was I then? 16, 15? So it was-- it was different then than now. People were not-- we're not developed. And we didn't-- I think we were more involved with everyday living that-- just, you know, to get, to get enough to eat. That was a-- that was a very important part of-- when you're hungry, that's very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food? Where did it come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we got [NON-ENGLISH]. And also, when we did go out of the ghetto to work, that's basically-- otherwise, we just got the rations, which was like this thin soup, and bread, and some milk. And this cake once a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did things start to change in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt never changed. Theresienstadt-- I mean, it changed, but I was no longer there. I understand it did change. But while I was there, it did not change, except we had an epidemic of encephalitis, and everybody ended up in the hospital. And I remember that I wanted to be in the hospital too. And I pretended that I had encephalitis, and I used to take the thermometer and rub it up to like, 101. And I was in the hospital, but I never caught it, ever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the hospital like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like an infirmary, but I just wanted to be with my friends. I didn't want to stay behind. But I never caught it, and I was miraculously recovered within a month. [LAUGHS] Everybody else took like, three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they went back to the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was medical-- how was the medical care during that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had Jewish physicians. We didn't have enough medication. We didn't have enough-- it was adequate. I have a scar on my hand now from-- I had an infection, and they had to cut. And it was-- I mean, it was OK. I survived it. I didn't get gangrene or anything like that. And then this deportation started. And that's when, you know, everything started, in Theresienstadt, to change as far as the tranquility was-- I mean, you, you started worrying about other things besides being hungry because your friends were being deported to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that start?

speaker: interviewee
text: '43 probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- did you know what Auschwitz was at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what Auschwitz was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard rumors that there were camps like that, but we didn't really know. No. We did not know it was an extermination camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did people think people were going when they were deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: To other camps because there was not enough room to make room for more-- see, Theresienstadt had, basically, more Czech Jews than Germans because it was in Czechoslovakia. So we were told that we had to make room for Hungarian Jews and all that. So this is why they started it. But, actually, the one-- when I was aware of it is when this Dr. Freudenthal and Anneliese, this woman, had to leave with the children's transport. And this was the first that it really hit me personally that there was such a thing as being deported and getting--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when they got deported? What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we, we were told that they were going to be saved. We didn't know that they were going to be gassed. That they were going to take the kids off and eventually send them to Israel to save the children. I mean, these were small children, orphans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many? You said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your sister during this? Was she still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in the home. You know, these were-- these were orphans, children who didn't have parents anymore. And then after-- shortly after that, family-- they started to deport other families for one reason or another. At that time, my father had gotten a job working for the post office, for some connections he had. And please-- I really don't know what it was anymore. And in the end of '43, he was caught stealing food out of a package that had fallen out or whatever. I don't blame him for stealing food because I-- I mean, anyone who's hungry and doesn't steal, if it's available, I think is an idiot because, I mean, really, it's very painful to be hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he then was told that because of that, he would have to be deported. And in '43, '44, the beginning of '44, he finally-- he was told that he has to leave. And my mother said, if he goes, we all go. So we all four of us went. What happened-- before stealing food, did you have to do a lot of things like that to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I stole from, from, from the farmers. Yeah. You could not survive on what was given to you. I mean, this-- people died of starvation. And somehow, I think a woman is able to do without food much more so than a man. Now, maybe they say that they have-- we have more fat cells, so they store more food. But I think it's more difficult for a man than a woman to be without food. Well, this is later on, so I don't want to talk about it now. But, I mean, it's just amazing what my mother could do. But if food were available, I would have taken it too. I mean, that's all-- if you-- you don't even think about it. I don't think about it as stealing. I mean, it's survival of the fittest. If there's food, you take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people were still mailing food in and all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- did you get mail regularly? Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't have-- we got-- No. We didn't have anyone left anymore to mail us things. Oh, my relatives from America occasionally sent packages. But then in '43, all of that stopped-- I think in '43. In '43. So there were no more packages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, though, you could correspond with them? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't get any, any mail from them. But I understand they got-- this is one of the letters that my father had sent. Was-- so they did get a letter from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- now when you got deported, what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I had to leave. And at that time-- [SIGHS] All my friends stayed. I was the first one to go. I think I was. Maybe one or two went, but for my close circle of friends, I was the first one to go. [SIGHS] This was the first experience I had going into a cattle car. And we squeezed like, 50 to 75 people in this one car with a bucket in the middle as a toilet. And it was winter. It was cold. It was early Spring, but it was still cold. And we traveled like that for like, two and a half days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my family, but I-- I just cried the whole time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your grandmother with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She had-- I had forgot to mentioned this. She had been deported to Auschwitz about three months before we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you hear from her, or see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. You don't hear from anyone anymore. Uh uh. We didn't even know where she was, and didn't even know she was in Auschwitz. We found her again. That's how I found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the cattle car. No-- well, I wouldn't eat. My mother and sister-- every-- people had food. I don't know where they got it from, chocolate and all kinds of stuff. I wouldn't eat a thing. I was just crying. I just didn't-- I didn't want to leave. I didn't want to leave my friends. I didn't want to go. And I just was utterly miserable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened? Where did you-- you went to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we, we got to Auschwitz, and the SS was walking up and down on the railway station, yelling commands. And there were dogs. And they opened up the doors. And they were yelling, get out. Get out. And, line up. Line up. And then we all lined up. And then there was a woman with a small child, and the kid started crying. And one of these SS men just took the kid out of the mother's arms, stepped on his foot-- on his head. And that was the first time I had ever seen anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would come from Theresienstadt on the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole train was full. A lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then they marched-- they did not separate us. They marched us all into Birkenau, which was the family camp. First, we got-- we got tattooed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was you number?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have my number. It's A-1712. Do you want to see it? But-- and then after the tattoo, we marched into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold still for a moment. Can you turn your hand? Turn it-- turn it that way more, the other way. The other way. No, the whole arm down. There we go. Hold-- one moment. And tell me the-- it's-- tell me the number please.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that tattooing like? I mean, what was that scene?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't care. It didn't hurt. It was a little like, a little needle pricking. At that point, I'd-- I don't think you-- you know, you become numb. You just don't care anymore. And I still was crying, and I still didn't want to be there. And then we went into this-- we were separated. My father went into one barrack, and my mother and my sister and I went into another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have your possessions or anything with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we did-- they did. No, we didn't. We just were allowed to keep a few, few things. They took all our jewelery and all that away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you keep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some clothing, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a winter coat? Or what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I had a jacket. Yeah. And I had some clothing. I mean, at that point I still had it. Eventually, I didn't have it anymore. And we went into this barrack and my-- triple bunk beds-- and my-- with a straw mattress. And my mother was downstairs. My sister was in the second tier. And  I was on the third tier. And we now got food once a day. We got a bowl of soup and a piece of bread, and that was it. And I, I didn't eat at that time. I still hadn't eaten. I remember-- you see, I, I had blamed my father for this a long time. I don't do anything now-- anymore now. But he came to visit me, and I gave him my food. And he ate it, which my mother would never do. But-- and I had blamed him for this for a-- for a long time. I don't do that anymore. After I went to the psychiatrist for years, somehow I, I don't do that anymore. But-- [SIGHS] --then we found out that my grandmother was on the verge of death. She was not gassed. She was-- she just-- she died of starvation like, a week after we got there. She was like-- she was a walking skeleton.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in this-- she happened to be in Birkenau in the same family camp that we ended up in. Also, there was the barbed wire, electrified all around it, which-- I mean, we-- I had-- Theresienstadt wasn't like that. The people-- the people were walking skeletons, everyone you saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you recognize your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even remember having seen her anymore. I don't think I saw her. I just was out of it. I was-- I just stayed on the bed. I didn't want to do anything. Then, then we were told about the smoke and this odor. And we found out about the gas chambers, which we didn't know. We did not know until we arrived in Birkenau. And the SS was constantly present, walking around, patrolling the area, kicking you in the behind if you weren't fast enough. I mean, you didn't have a moment's peace anymore. It was just-- and then Dita came to Birkenau, and I-- and somehow, my life changed again because she was-- she was the closest friend. And then my parents got-- my mother got us-- we went into a youth home again. Even in the Auschwitz, they had a special home for children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like? What were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much better, but a little better. I think we got a little more food, a little more soup and a little bit bigger piece of bread. But there were little, little kids there, as well as bigger kids. And the little children didn't-- they were orphans. They didn't want-- they wanted to be with their families, their mothers. They didn't want to be by themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, the bigger ones, we had to take care of the little ones, which kept us busy and we did that. And basically, the whole time your whole emphasis is on food, trying to get something to eat. How did you get things to eat at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't. We just got the ration. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to when you got there, what was-- when you first arrived, did they shower you? What type of process was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't-- we didn't-- we weren't showered.

speaker: interviewer
text: You kept your clothes at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were tattooed. We kept our clothes. But, again, I understand that we were privileged to go into the family camp. Usually people are separated and gassed when they arrived. They didn't do that with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you had been at Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see your mother a lot then during the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw her once a day. And I saw my father once a day. And I used to yell at my sister because my mother didn't eat anything anymore. She just gave her food to us. And most of the time, I didn't take it. And my sister took it. And sometimes I took it. I don't know. So-- but she became thinner, and thinner, and thinner. She turned into a skeleton, like my grandma, right in front of my eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the days like there? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, took care of the other little kids. We spent a lot of time with children. We taught them things. We played with them. We walked with them. I didn't have a job, not while I was-- I wasn't there long. And, and-- but while I was there, I did not work. I was in Auschwitz maybe six weeks at the most.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw your father the whole time there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw him three, four times a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing during there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just hanging out. I think that's the-- that's the hard part too for people who have been active, and just doing nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked about the SS officers. What were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were just strutting up and down, like saying, well, I mean, we are the masters. You are the slaves. And, you know, it's just-- if you didn't do anything, they-- you ignored them, they ignored you. But if they wanted you to do something, you weren't fast enough to do it, you would know about it. But actually, the only-- in Auschwitz, the only abuse I saw was when the SS man stepped, stepped on the kid. But other than that, I-- I mean, but something like somebody kicks you in the rear end or something like that, but nothing else like that. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked about the smoke. Did you smell it and see?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw the smoke. We smelled the smoke. And when, when we found out what it was, everybody became petrified that they would be part of the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anytime-- if you had-- it happened to me later on, but you took of shower, nobody wanted to take a shower of course because-- I-- how did we wash? Oh, we have used-- we used-- I think we had a sink, and we used to wash. How did you find out about the smoke and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- people just talk about it, and you find out about it. And we had a latrine for a toilet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, outside of the barracks. We walked. And the, the worst thing was the hunger. And, well, you-- you no longer-- after a while, you lose your human dignity to some extent because you just--

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't care anymore. Most people. I never, I never gave up. But most people just don't-- they don't care anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with Dita the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like to have your friend there? That made it better?

speaker: interviewee
text: That made life different, yeah. We used to-- we never-- we used to pretend that we were in different places. We used to play games of mental telepathy, what, what we'll do when we get out. Fantasize about things, and share things, and care for each other. And we left Auschwitz together too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't we stop the tape here?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
